questions.prompt: Which planet is known as the 'Red Planet'?
questions.funFact: Mars gets its reddish color from iron oxide (rust) covering its surface.

questions.prompt: Which gas do humans exhale in a greater amount than they inhale?
questions.funFact: Your body uses inhaled oxygen to convert food into energy, releasing extra carbon dioxide as a byproduct.

questions.prompt: Which part of the cell is known as the 'powerhouse of the cell'?
questions.funFact: Mitochondria have their own small set of DNA, separate from the DNA in the cell's nucleus.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a state of matter?
questions.funFact: Plasma is actually the most common state of matter in the visible universe, found inside stars.

questions.prompt: What force keeps the planets in orbit around the Sun?
questions.funFact: The same gravity that keeps you on the ground also keeps every planet locked in its orbit.

questions.prompt: Which blood type is known as the 'universal donor'?
questions.funFact: O-negative blood lacks the antigens that most commonly trigger an immune reaction in recipients.

questions.prompt: What do you call an animal that eats only plants?
questions.funFact: The word 'herbivore' comes from Latin: 'herba' (plant) and 'vorare' (to devour).

questions.prompt: Which sense organ is primarily responsible for your sense of balance?
questions.funFact: Tiny fluid-filled canals in the inner ear detect rotation and movement to help you stay balanced.

questions.prompt: What is the boiling point of water in Celsius at sea level?
questions.funFact: At higher altitudes, water boils at a lower temperature because air pressure is lower.

questions.prompt: Which organ produces insulin in the human body?
questions.funFact: Insulin, isolated in 1921, was one of the first hormones ever used as a medical treatment.

questions.prompt: What type of chemical bond forms when atoms share electrons?
questions.funFact: The hydrogen and oxygen atoms inside a single water molecule are held together by covalent bonds.

questions.prompt: Which type of electromagnetic radiation has the shortest wavelength?
questions.funFact: Gamma rays are produced by some of the most energetic events in the universe, like supernova explosions.

questions.prompt: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
questions.funFact: 'Au' comes from 'aurum', the Latin word for gold.

questions.prompt: Which blood vessels carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart?
questions.funFact: The pulmonary artery is a notable exception, carrying oxygen-poor blood to the lungs.

questions.prompt: What is the process called in which plants lose water vapor through their leaves?
questions.funFact: A large oak tree can release hundreds of liters of water into the air through transpiration on a hot day.

questions.prompt: Which subatomic particle carries no electric charge?
questions.funFact: The neutron was discovered by physicist James Chadwick in 1932.

questions.prompt: What is the scientific study of fungi called?
questions.funFact: A mushroom is just the fruiting body of a much larger fungal network living underground.

questions.prompt: What is the most abundant element in the human body by mass?
questions.funFact: Oxygen makes up about 65% of your body's mass, mostly bound up in water molecules.

questions.prompt: Which layer of the Earth is mainly responsible for generating Earth's magnetic field?
questions.funFact: The outer core is made of swirling liquid iron and nickel, and its motion generates the geomagnetic field.

questions.prompt: Which scientist proposed the theory of general relativity?
questions.funFact: General relativity, published in 1915, redefined gravity as the curvature of spacetime itself.

questions.prompt: In genetics, what term describes an organism's observable physical characteristics?
questions.funFact: Identical genotypes can still produce different phenotypes depending on environmental factors.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the process by which stars generate their energy?
questions.funFact: In the Sun's core, hydrogen atoms fuse into helium, releasing the energy that lights up our solar system.

questions.prompt: Which unit is used to measure electrical resistance?
questions.funFact: The ohm is named after German physicist Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.prompt: In chemistry, what term describes a compound that donates hydrogen ions in solution?
questions.funFact: This definition of an acid comes from the Bronsted-Lowry theory of acids and bases.

questions.prompt: Which vitamin does human skin produce when exposed to sunlight?
questions.funFact: Vitamin D made in the skin is essential for helping your body absorb calcium for strong bones.

questions.prompt: What term describes two species evolving in response to each other over time?
questions.funFact: A classic example of coevolution is a flower and its specific pollinator developing matched shapes together.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing can escape?
questions.funFact: The first-ever image of a black hole's event horizon, of the galaxy M87, was released in 2019.

questions.prompt: Which ancient civilization built Machu Picchu?
questions.funFact: Machu Picchu was built in the 15th century and stayed largely unknown to the outside world until 1911.

questions.prompt: Who was the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic Ocean?
questions.funFact: Earhart completed her solo transatlantic flight in 1932, five years after Charles Lindbergh's famous crossing.

questions.prompt: Which empire was ruled by Genghis Khan?
questions.funFact: At its height, the Mongol Empire became the largest contiguous land empire in history.

questions.prompt: In which country did the Renaissance begin?
questions.funFact: The Renaissance took root in Florence in the 14th century before spreading across Europe.

questions.prompt: Which South African leader spent 27 years in prison before becoming president?
questions.funFact: Mandela became South Africa's first Black president in 1994, after the end of apartheid.

questions.prompt: Which ancient wonder of the world stood in the harbor of Alexandria, Egypt?
questions.funFact: The Lighthouse of Alexandria stood for centuries and is counted among the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

questions.prompt: Which document did King John of England sign in 1215, limiting royal power?
questions.funFact: Magna Carta established the principle that even the king was subject to the law.

questions.prompt: Which ancient Greek philosopher personally tutored Alexander the Great?
questions.funFact: Aristotle tutored young Alexander before he went on to conquer much of the known world.

questions.prompt: Which wall divided a European capital city from 1961 to 1989?
questions.funFact: The Berlin Wall physically divided East and West Berlin for 28 years.

questions.prompt: Which Chinese dynasty commissioned the famous Terracotta Army?
questions.funFact: The Terracotta Army was built to guard the tomb of Qin Shi Huang, China's first emperor.

questions.prompt: The Rosetta Stone, key to decoding Egyptian hieroglyphs, was inscribed in how many scripts?
questions.funFact: The Rosetta Stone carries the same decree in hieroglyphic, Demotic, and ancient Greek.

questions.prompt: Which modern country was formerly known internationally as Persia?
questions.funFact: The country officially asked the world to use the name 'Iran' instead of 'Persia' starting in 1935.

questions.prompt: Which present-day country received the largest number of enslaved Africans transported across the Atlantic?
questions.funFact: Historians estimate that millions of enslaved Africans were transported to Brazil, far more than to any other single destination in the Americas.

questions.prompt: Which war was fought between the northern and southern United States from 1861 to 1865?
questions.funFact: The American Civil War remains the deadliest conflict in U.S. history in terms of American lives lost.

questions.prompt: Who became the first emperor of the Roman Empire?
questions.funFact: Augustus, born Octavian, became emperor in 27 BCE after defeating Mark Antony and Cleopatra.

questions.prompt: Which explorer led the first expedition to circumnavigate the globe, though he died before it was completed?
questions.funFact: Magellan was killed in the Philippines in 1521; his surviving crew completed the voyage without him.

questions.prompt: In which year did the Great Fire of London occur?
questions.funFact: The fire destroyed much of medieval London but led to major rebuilding, including a new St. Paul's Cathedral.

questions.prompt: Which Indian leader is best known for leading nonviolent resistance against British rule?
questions.funFact: Gandhi's philosophy of nonviolent civil disobedience later influenced leaders like Martin Luther King Jr.

questions.prompt: The Byzantine Empire was the continuation of which earlier empire?
questions.funFact: The Byzantine Empire's capital, Constantinople, finally fell in 1453, marking its end.

questions.prompt: Which 1648 peace agreement is often credited with establishing the modern concept of state sovereignty?
questions.funFact: The Peace of Westphalia ended the devastating Thirty Years' War in Europe.

questions.prompt: Which female pharaoh ruled Egypt for about two decades and is often depicted wearing a ceremonial false beard?
questions.funFact: Hatshepsut ruled during Egypt's 18th dynasty and was one of the few female pharaohs to reign in her own right.

questions.prompt: The Meiji Restoration of 1868 marked the start of rapid modernization in which country?
questions.funFact: The Meiji Restoration ended centuries of feudal shogunate rule and set Japan on a path to industrialization.

questions.prompt: Which West African empire controlled a major share of the medieval trans-Saharan gold and salt trade?
questions.funFact: Mansa Musa of the Mali Empire is often cited by historians as one of the wealthiest individuals in history.

questions.prompt: Which 1917 revolution led to the collapse of the Russian monarchy?
questions.funFact: Tsar Nicholas II abdicated in February 1917, ending over three centuries of Romanov rule.

questions.prompt: Which ancient Babylonian legal code is one of the earliest known written sets of laws?
questions.funFact: The Code of Hammurabi, dating to about 1754 BCE, includes the principle of 'an eye for an eye.'

questions.prompt: The Congress of Vienna of 1814-1815 aimed to redraw the map of Europe after the defeat of which leader?
questions.funFact: The Congress of Vienna sought to restore a balance of power in Europe after decades of Napoleonic wars.

questions.prompt: Which dynasty ruled China when the first Portuguese traders arrived in the early 16th century?
questions.funFact: The Ming dynasty ruled China from 1368 to 1644 and built much of the Great Wall as it stands today.

questions.prompt: What is the capital of Australia?
questions.funFact: Canberra was purpose-built as a compromise capital between rival cities Sydney and Melbourne.

questions.prompt: Which country is famously shaped like a boot on the map?
questions.funFact: Italy's boot shape comes from the Apennine Peninsula extending into the Mediterranean Sea.

questions.prompt: Which desert covers much of northern China and southern Mongolia?
questions.funFact: The Gobi is one of the coldest deserts in the world, with brutally freezing winters.

questions.prompt: What is the smallest continent by land area?
questions.funFact: Australia is both the smallest continent and the largest island in the world.

questions.prompt: Which South American country is named after the independence leader Simon Bolivar?
questions.funFact: Bolivia gained independence in 1825 and named itself in Bolivar's honor.

questions.prompt: Which U.S. state is made up entirely of islands?
questions.funFact: Hawaii is a chain of volcanic islands formed over a hot spot in the Pacific tectonic plate.

questions.prompt: Which river flows through the city of Paris, France?
questions.funFact: The Seine winds for roughly 777 kilometers before emptying into the English Channel.

questions.prompt: Which country has coastline on both the Atlantic and Indian Oceans, meeting near Africa's southern tip?
questions.funFact: Cape Agulhas, not the more famous Cape of Good Hope, is technically where the Atlantic and Indian Oceans meet.

questions.prompt: Which country straddles both Europe and Asia along the Bosphorus Strait?
questions.funFact: Istanbul is the only major city in the world located on two different continents.

questions.prompt: What is the capital city of Canada?
questions.funFact: Ottawa was chosen as capital partly for its location straddling English and French Canada.

questions.prompt: Which African country was never formally colonized by a European power, aside from a brief occupation?
questions.funFact: Ethiopia famously defeated an invading Italian army at the Battle of Adwa in 1896.

questions.prompt: Which sea is so salty and dense that swimmers float easily on its surface?
questions.funFact: The Dead Sea's shoreline is the lowest point on Earth's land surface.

questions.prompt: Which mountain range is traditionally used to divide Europe from Asia within Russia?
questions.funFact: The Ural Mountains run roughly north to south and form one of the classic continental boundary lines.

questions.prompt: Which country has the most time zones of any nation, thanks to its overseas territories?
questions.funFact: Because of territories scattered around the globe, France spans about 12 time zones.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the line of longitude at 0 degrees, from which time zones are measured?
questions.funFact: The Prime Meridian passes directly through Greenwich, England.

questions.prompt: Which strait separates Asia from North America at its narrowest point?
questions.funFact: At its narrowest, the Bering Strait is only about 82 kilometers wide, separating Russia and Alaska.

questions.prompt: Which Southeast Asian country consists of more than 7,000 islands?
questions.funFact: Only about 2,000 of the Philippines' thousands of islands are inhabited.

questions.prompt: What is the driest nonpolar desert on Earth, located in South America?
questions.funFact: Parts of the Atacama Desert have gone decades without any recorded rainfall.

questions.prompt: Which landlocked European country is famous for the Matterhorn and precision watchmaking?
questions.funFact: Switzerland has maintained a policy of neutrality and did not fight as a belligerent in either World War.

questions.prompt: Which African lake is the primary source of the White Nile?
questions.funFact: Lake Victoria is Africa's largest lake by surface area and is shared by Kenya, Uganda, and Tanzania.

questions.prompt: Which still-active volcano near Naples, Italy, destroyed the city of Pompeii in 79 CE?
questions.funFact: Vesuvius is considered one of the most dangerous volcanoes today because of the dense population living nearby.

questions.prompt: Which tectonic boundary runs through Iceland, splitting it between two continental plates?
questions.funFact: Iceland is one of the few places on Earth where you can walk across this oceanic ridge above sea level.

questions.prompt: Which country is entirely enclosed by South Africa, making it a landlocked enclave?
questions.funFact: Lesotho is one of only a handful of countries in the world completely surrounded by a single other nation, alongside San Marino and Vatican City, which are both enclaved within Italy.

questions.prompt: Which body of water, technically a lake, is the largest inland body of water in the world by surface area?
questions.funFact: The Caspian Sea is bordered by five countries: Russia, Kazakhstan, Turkmenistan, Iran, and Azerbaijan.

questions.prompt: Which two countries share the longest international land border in the world?
questions.funFact: The US-Canada border stretches over 8,800 kilometers once Alaska is included.

questions.prompt: Which remote Pacific island, a territory of Chile, is famous for its giant stone statues called moai?
questions.funFact: The moai statues were carved by the Rapa Nui people, most likely between the 13th and 16th centuries.

questions.prompt: Which strait, running through the middle of Istanbul, connects the Black Sea to the Sea of Marmara?
questions.funFact: The Bosphorus is one of the busiest and narrowest straits used for international navigation anywhere in the world.

questions.prompt: What comes next in the sequence: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ...?
questions.funFact: These are perfect squares — each number is some integer multiplied by itself.

questions.prompt: If all roses are flowers, and some flowers fade quickly, can we conclude that some roses fade quickly?
choices: Only if the roses are red
questions.funFact: This is a classic invalid syllogism — the 'some flowers' that fade quickly might not include any roses at all.

questions.prompt: Which number is missing: 2, 5, 8, 11, __, 17?
questions.funFact: Each number in the sequence increases by exactly 3.

questions.prompt: A farmer has 17 sheep, and all but 9 die. How many sheep are left?
questions.funFact: 'All but 9' means 9 survive — it's easy to misread the phrase and subtract the wrong number.

questions.prompt: Which word doesn't belong with the others: Piano, Guitar, Violin, Radio?
questions.funFact: Piano, guitar, and violin are musical instruments; a radio only plays sound, it doesn't create it.

questions.prompt: If today is Sunday, what day was it 3 days ago?
questions.funFact: Counting backward from Sunday three days lands you on Thursday.

questions.prompt: Mary's mother has four children. Three are named April, May, and June. What is the fourth child's name?
questions.funFact: This classic riddle tricks people into guessing another month, forgetting the question already named the fourth child.

questions.prompt: Complete the pattern: circle, square, circle, square, circle, ...?
questions.funFact: The pattern simply alternates back and forth between two shapes.

questions.prompt: You have two coins totaling 30 cents, and one of them is not a 5-cent coin. What are the two coins?
questions.funFact: The trick is that only ONE coin isn't a 5-cent piece — the other one still can be.

questions.prompt: Which shape has no corners at all?
questions.funFact: A circle is a closed curve where every point is equally distant from the center, with no straight edges.

questions.prompt: A clock shows 3:15. What is the angle between the hour and minute hands?
questions.funFact: Many people assume the hands line up exactly at 3:15, but the hour hand has already crept slightly past the 3.

questions.prompt: What is the next number: 1, 2, 4, 7, 11, 16, ...?
questions.funFact: The gaps between consecutive numbers grow by one each time: +1, +2, +3, +4, +5, +6.

questions.prompt: Three switches outside a room control three bulbs inside, and you can only enter the room once. How can you determine which switch operates which bulb?
choices: Turn on one switch, wait a while, turn it off, turn on a second switch, then check the bulbs' heat and state inside
Ask someone else to check for you
Flip all switches at once and guess
questions.funFact: The trick uses heat as a hidden third clue — a bulb that's off but still warm belonged to the first switch you used.

questions.prompt: If it takes 5 machines 5 minutes to make 5 widgets, how long would it take 100 machines to make 100 widgets?
questions.funFact: Since each machine independently makes one widget in 5 minutes, adding more machines running in parallel doesn't change that time.

questions.prompt: Which number continues the pattern: 3, 9, 27, 81, ...?
questions.funFact: Each term in this sequence is 3 raised to an increasing power.

questions.prompt: A man looks at a portrait and says: 'Brothers and sisters, I have none, but this man's father is my father's son.' Whose portrait is he looking at?
questions.funFact: 'My father's son,' with no siblings, must be the man himself — so the portrait shows his own son.

questions.prompt: Two trains, 60 km apart, move toward each other at 30 km/h each. How long until they meet?
questions.funFact: Their combined closing speed is 60 km/h, so together they cover the 60 km gap in exactly one hour.

questions.prompt: If some cats are black, and all black things absorb more heat, what can we conclude about those cats?
questions.funFact: Unlike arguments that wrongly stretch 'some' into 'all,' this is a genuinely valid logical deduction.

questions.prompt: A cat and a fish together weigh 12 kg. The cat weighs exactly 4 times as much as the fish. How much does the fish weigh?
questions.funFact: Setting up a simple equation (x + 4x = 12) instantly reveals the hidden proportions in a puzzle like this.

questions.prompt: You have two ropes, each of which takes exactly 60 minutes to burn completely, but neither burns at a consistent rate along its length. How can you measure exactly 45 minutes using only these ropes and matches?
choices: Burn one rope from both ends and the other rope from one end at the same time, then light the second rope's other end the moment the first rope finishes burning
Burn both ropes from one end only
Cut each rope in half and burn the pieces
questions.funFact: Burning a rope from both ends always finishes it in exactly half its total time, no matter how unevenly it burns.

questions.prompt: In a room of 30 people, everyone shakes hands with everyone else exactly once. How many total handshakes occur?
questions.funFact: This classic combinatorics problem is solved with the formula n(n-1)/2, here giving 30 x 29 / 2 = 435.

questions.prompt: A man says, 'Everything I say is a lie.' Is this statement true or false?
questions.funFact: This is a variant of the classic liar's paradox, where the statement can't be consistently labeled true or false.

questions.prompt: A snail at the bottom of a 10-meter well climbs 3 meters every day but slides back 2 meters every night. How many days does it take to reach the top?
questions.funFact: Because the snail escapes as soon as it reaches the top during the day, you don't count a final slide back — a classic trap in this riddle.

questions.prompt: In a family of 6 children, exactly half are boys. If two more girls are born into the family, what fraction of the children will be girls?
questions.funFact: Starting with 3 girls and 3 boys, adding 2 more girls makes 5 girls out of 8 total children.

questions.prompt: If you rearrange the letters 'CIFAIPC', you get the name of a:
questions.funFact: Anagram puzzles like this one train your brain to spot patterns hidden inside scrambled letters — here, 'PACIFIC'.

questions.prompt: A is the mother of B, but B is not the daughter of A. How is this possible?
questions.funFact: This riddle plays on the hidden assumption that 'child' must default to 'daughter,' when B could simply be a son.

questions.prompt: Using only an unmarked 3-liter jug and an unmarked 5-liter jug, how can you measure out exactly 4 liters of water?
choices: It's impossible with these two jugs
Fill the 5L jug, pour into the 3L jug until full, empty the 3L jug, pour the remaining 2L into the 3L jug, refill the 5L jug, then top off the 3L jug — leaving exactly 4L in the 5L jug
Fill the 3L jug twice into the 5L jug
Fill both jugs and add their volumes together
questions.funFact: This is the classic 'water jug' puzzle, later made famous by a scene in the movie Die Hard with a Vengeance.

questions.prompt: Which streaming service originally produced 'Stranger Things'?
questions.funFact: Stranger Things premiered in 2016 and became one of Netflix's most-watched original series.

questions.prompt: How many members were in the classic lineup of The Beatles?
questions.funFact: John, Paul, George, and Ringo made up the Beatles' iconic lineup.

questions.prompt: What is Spider-Man's secret identity?
questions.funFact: Peter Parker first appeared in Amazing Fantasy #15 in 1962.

questions.prompt: In Disney's 'Frozen,' what power does Elsa have?
questions.funFact: Frozen's song 'Let It Go' won the Academy Award for Best Original Song.

questions.prompt: Which social media platform was famous for short posts originally called 'tweets'?
questions.funFact: Twitter rebranded itself as X in 2023.

questions.prompt: What color is Kermit the Frog?
questions.funFact: Kermit was created by puppeteer Jim Henson and first appeared on television in 1955.

questions.prompt: Which video game character is a mustached plumber who jumps through green pipes?
questions.funFact: Mario made his debut in 1981's 'Donkey Kong,' where he was originally called Jumpman.

questions.prompt: In 'The Wizard of Oz,' what color are Dorothy's famous shoes?
questions.funFact: In L. Frank Baum's original book, the shoes were silver, not red.

questions.prompt: Which fast-food chain uses golden arches as its logo?
questions.funFact: The golden arches are considered one of the most recognized logos in the world.

questions.prompt: Which animation studio created 'Toy Story'?
questions.funFact: Toy Story (1995) was the first entirely computer-animated feature film.

questions.prompt: Which director is best known for 1975's 'Jaws,' often called the first summer blockbuster?
questions.funFact: Jaws' massive success helped establish the modern wide-release summer movie strategy.

questions.prompt: Who directed the 1997 film 'Titanic'?
questions.funFact: Titanic was the first film to gross over $1 billion worldwide.

questions.prompt: Which British rock band performed 'Bohemian Rhapsody'?
questions.funFact: Bohemian Rhapsody famously blends rock, opera, and ballad styles in a single six-minute song.

questions.prompt: In what year was the first iPhone released?
questions.funFact: Steve Jobs unveiled the first iPhone at Macworld in January 2007.

questions.prompt: Which short-video app, originally launched in China as Douyin, became a global phenomenon?
questions.funFact: TikTok's international version launched in 2017 after merging with the app Musical.ly.

questions.prompt: Which pop icon released the best-selling album 'Thriller' in 1982?
questions.funFact: Thriller remains one of the best-selling albums of all time.

questions.prompt: In 'The Simpsons,' what is the name of the family's pet dog?
questions.funFact: The Simpson family adopted the greyhound in the very first episode, 'Simpsons Roasting on an Open Fire.'

questions.prompt: Which award show, nicknamed the 'Emmy,' honors achievements in television?
questions.funFact: The Emmy statuette depicts a winged woman holding an atom, symbolizing the art and science of television.

questions.prompt: Which company created the PlayStation video game console?
questions.funFact: The original PlayStation launched in Japan in December 1994.

questions.prompt: As of the mid-2020s, which artist holds the record for the most Grammy Awards won by any individual?
questions.funFact: Beyoncé surpassed conductor Georg Solti's long-standing record in 2023.

questions.prompt: Which 1994 film, starring Tom Hanks, follows a man with a low IQ living through pivotal moments in American history?
questions.funFact: Forrest Gump won the Academy Award for Best Picture in 1995.

questions.prompt: Prince starred in and released the album for which semi-autobiographical 1984 film?
questions.funFact: The title track 'Purple Rain' became one of Prince's signature songs.

questions.prompt: Which sitcom is set largely in a coffeehouse called Central Perk?
questions.funFact: Friends ran for ten seasons, from 1994 to 2004.

questions.prompt: The video game character 'Master Chief' is the protagonist of which sci-fi franchise?
questions.funFact: Halo: Combat Evolved launched alongside the original Xbox in 2001.

questions.prompt: Which massive 1969 outdoor music festival took place on a dairy farm in Bethel, New York?
questions.funFact: Woodstock drew an estimated 400,000 attendees despite forecasts of much smaller crowds.

questions.prompt: Which brand's iconic advertising slogan is 'Just Do It'?
questions.funFact: Nike's 'Just Do It' slogan debuted in 1988 and became one of advertising's most recognized taglines.

questions.prompt: Which streaming platform released the record-breaking series 'Squid Game' in 2021?
questions.funFact: Squid Game became one of Netflix's most-watched series ever within weeks of release.

questions.prompt: What is 9 × 6?
Which of these numbers is prime?
questions.funFact: A prime number has exactly two factors: 1 and itself.

questions.prompt: What is 100 − 37?
How many sides does a hexagon have?
questions.funFact: 'Hexa' comes from the Greek word for six.

questions.prompt: What is half of 50?
questions.funFact: Half of 50 is 25, since 25×2=50.

questions.prompt: What is 3 cubed (3³)?
Which of these numbers is odd?
questions.funFact: Odd numbers can't be divided evenly by 2.

questions.prompt: How many degrees are in a right angle?
questions.funFact: A right angle looks like the corner of a square.

questions.prompt: What is 8 + 8 + 8?
questions.funFact: 8+8+8 is the same as 8×3=24.

questions.prompt: What is 144 divided by 12?
What is 25% of 80?
questions.funFact: 25% is the same as one-quarter, and a quarter of 80 is 20.

questions.prompt: What is the square root of 81?
What is 5 × (3 + 4)?
questions.funFact: Solve inside the parentheses first: 3+4=7, then 5×7=35.

questions.prompt: What is 2/3 + 1/6?
A rectangle has a length of 8 and a width of 5. What is its area?
questions.funFact: Area of a rectangle = length × width, so 8×5=40.

questions.prompt: What is 17 × 3?
What is 6² − 4²?
What is 3/4 expressed as a percentage?
questions.funFact: To convert a fraction to a percentage, divide the top by the bottom and multiply by 100.

questions.prompt: What comes next in this sequence: 2, 4, 8, 16, __?
questions.funFact: Each number in the sequence doubles the one before it.

questions.prompt: What is 15% of 340?
Solve for x: 3x + 7 = 22.
questions.funFact: Subtract 7 from both sides to get 3x=15, then divide by 3 to get x=5.

questions.prompt: What is 7! ÷ 5! (seven factorial divided by five factorial)?
questions.funFact: 7!÷5! simplifies to 7×6=42, since the 5! terms cancel out.

questions.prompt: What is the sum of the interior angles of a hexagon?
questions.funFact: The formula (n−2)×180° gives (6−2)×180°=720° for a hexagon.

questions.prompt: What is the square root of 225?
What is 2 raised to the power of 8 (2⁸)?
questions.funFact: 2⁸=256, a number familiar to anyone who's worked with computer bytes.

questions.prompt: A train travels at 60 mph for 2.5 hours. How far does it go?
questions.funFact: Distance = speed × time, so 60×2.5=150 miles.

questions.prompt: What is the least common multiple (LCM) of 4 and 6?
questions.funFact: 12 is the smallest number that both 4 and 6 divide into evenly.

questions.prompt: In which direction is Arabic traditionally written and read?
questions.funFact: This right-to-left direction is shared with other Semitic-family scripts like Hebrew.

questions.prompt: The English word 'coffee' is widely believed to come from which Arabic word?
questions.funFact: 'Qahwa' traveled through Turkish and Italian before becoming 'coffee' in English.

questions.prompt: The math term 'algebra' comes from the Arabic 'al-jabr,' found in the title of a book by which scholar?
questions.funFact: Al-Khwarizmi's 9th-century book title also gave us the word 'algorithm.'

questions.prompt: About how many letters are in the Arabic alphabet?
questions.funFact: Arabic has no separate capital letters, unlike the Latin alphabet.

questions.prompt: Arabic belongs to which family of languages?
questions.funFact: Hebrew and Aramaic are also Semitic languages, related to Arabic.

questions.prompt: What does the Arabic greeting 'Salaam' mean?
questions.funFact: The full greeting 'As-salamu alaykum' means 'peace be upon you.'

questions.prompt: The English word 'sofa' comes from an Arabic word describing what?
questions.funFact: The Arabic 'suffah' referred to a bench-like platform, often covered with cushions.

questions.prompt: Unlike Latin script, Arabic script has no distinction between which of the following?
questions.funFact: Arabic letters instead change shape depending on their position within a word.

questions.prompt: Which of these English words did NOT come from Arabic?
questions.funFact: 'Pizza' comes from Italian, while the other three all trace back to Arabic roots.

questions.prompt: Roughly how many countries have Arabic as an official language?
questions.funFact: The Arab League has 22 member states, most of which list Arabic as an official language.

questions.prompt: The word 'algorithm' comes from a Latinized version of the name of which mathematician?
questions.funFact: Al-Khwarizmi worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad in the 9th century.

questions.prompt: How are individual letters typically connected within a written Arabic word?
choices: They are always separated by spaces
They are joined together in a cursive style
They are written in blocks like Chinese characters
questions.funFact: Most Arabic letters change shape depending on whether they appear at the start, middle, or end of a word.

questions.prompt: The English word for the numeral '0,' 'cipher,' comes from the Arabic word 'sifr,' meaning what?
questions.funFact: 'Sifr' also gave rise to the word 'zero' through Italian and French.

questions.prompt: The English word 'giraffe' is believed to come from the Arabic 'zarafa,' referring to what?
questions.funFact: The word passed into English through Italian and French versions of the Arabic term.

questions.prompt: In Arabic, most words are built from a set of core consonants that carry the base meaning. What are these called?
questions.funFact: For example, the root k-t-b relates to writing, giving words like 'kitab' (book) and 'maktab' (office).

questions.prompt: The chess term 'checkmate' comes from a Persian-Arabic phrase, 'shah mat,' which roughly means what?
questions.funFact: Chess itself traveled to Europe through the Islamic world before reaching medieval Spain.

questions.prompt: The English word 'magazine' comes from the Arabic 'makhazin,' meaning what?
questions.funFact: The word first referred to storage places before coming to mean a printed periodical.

questions.prompt: What are the small marks placed above or below Arabic letters to indicate short vowel sounds called?
questions.funFact: Everyday Arabic writing, like newspapers, is usually printed without these marks.

questions.prompt: Arabic is one of how many official languages of the United Nations?
questions.funFact: The other five official UN languages are English, French, Spanish, Russian, and Chinese.

questions.prompt: The English word 'admiral' traces back to an Arabic phrase meaning roughly what?
questions.funFact: The phrase 'amir al-bahr' passed into medieval Latin and eventually into English.

questions.prompt: Which angular, geometric style of Arabic calligraphy was among the earliest used to copy the Quran?
questions.funFact: Kufic script gets its name from the city of Kufa in present-day Iraq.

questions.prompt: The English word 'hazard' is believed to derive from an Arabic word for what?
questions.funFact: The Arabic 'az-zahr' referred to dice used in games of chance, which carried an element of risk.

questions.prompt: The English word 'assassin' is historically linked to an Arabic term referring to users of what substance?
questions.funFact: The term dates back to a medieval sect in the Middle East and later entered European languages via the Crusades.

questions.prompt: Modern Standard Arabic is based primarily on the classical language of which text?
questions.funFact: The Quran's 7th-century language helped standardize classical Arabic grammar and vocabulary.

questions.prompt: The English word 'average,' in its original trade sense, passed into English from Arabic through which intermediary language?
questions.funFact: It traces back to Arabic 'awariya' (damaged goods) via the Italian trading term 'avaria.'

questions.prompt: Hindu-Arabic numerals, the digits used worldwide today, originated in which region before spreading through the Arab world to Europe?
questions.funFact: Arab mathematicians helped transmit these numerals to medieval Europe, which is why they're often called 'Arabic numerals.'

questions.prompt: The English word 'monsoon' comes from the Arabic 'mawsim' (season), passed on through which seafaring language?
questions.funFact: Portuguese traders in the Indian Ocean adopted the term for the seasonal winds that shaped sailing schedules.

questions.prompt: Which punctuation mark is used at the end of a question?
questions.funFact: The question mark likely evolved from a Latin abbreviation for 'quaestio,' meaning 'question.'

questions.prompt: What do we call a word that means nearly the same as another word?
questions.funFact: 'Happy' and 'joyful' are synonyms of each other.

questions.prompt: Complete the proverb: 'The early bird catches the ___.'
questions.funFact: The proverb encourages starting tasks early to gain an advantage.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a proper noun?
questions.funFact: Proper nouns name specific people, places, or things and are always capitalized.

questions.prompt: What part of speech describes an action, like 'run' or 'jump'?
questions.funFact: Every complete sentence in English needs at least one verb.

questions.prompt: Which of these words is spelled correctly?
questions.funFact: The rule 'i before e, except after c' helps with this word, though English has plenty of exceptions.

questions.prompt: What is the plural form of 'foot'?
questions.funFact: 'Feet' is an irregular plural, formed by changing the vowel rather than adding '-s.'

questions.prompt: Which word means the opposite of 'begin'?
questions.funFact: Words with opposite meanings are called antonyms.

questions.prompt: In the sentence 'I saw a cat,' which word is the subject?
questions.funFact: The subject of a sentence is who or what performs the action of the verb.

questions.prompt: Which punctuation mark shows possession, as in 'Sam's book'?
questions.funFact: Apostrophes are also used to form contractions, like 'don't' for 'do not.'

questions.prompt: What figure of speech compares two unlike things using 'like' or 'as'?
questions.funFact: 'As brave as a lion' is a classic example of a simile.

questions.prompt: Which of these is a homophone of 'their'?
questions.funFact: 'Their,' 'there,' and 'they're' all sound alike but have completely different meanings.

questions.prompt: What do we call a word that reads the same forward and backward, like 'level' or 'radar'?
questions.funFact: Long natural palindrome words are rare, since long ones are hard to form naturally.

questions.prompt: The famous line 'To be or not to be' comes from which Shakespeare play?
questions.funFact: The line opens a soliloquy in which Hamlet contemplates life and death.

questions.prompt: What is the correct plural form of 'cactus'?
questions.funFact: 'Cacti' comes from the word's Latin/Greek origin, though 'cactuses' is also accepted in modern English.

questions.prompt: In grammar, what do we call the base, unconjugated form of a verb, such as 'to run'?
questions.funFact: Infinitives are often used after other verbs, as in 'I want to run.'

questions.prompt: Which word is an antonym of 'generous'?
questions.funFact: 'Stingy' describes someone unwilling to give or spend.

questions.prompt: What figure of speech gives human qualities to non-human things, as in 'the wind whispered'?
questions.funFact: Personification is common in poetry to make descriptions feel more vivid.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses correct subject-verb agreement?
questions.funFact: Singular subjects like 'she' take a verb ending in '-s' in the present simple tense.

questions.prompt: Which letter of the alphabet does not appear in the name of any U.S. state?
questions.funFact: Not a single U.S. state name contains the letter Q.

questions.prompt: Which real English word, 15 letters long, is famously cited as the longest common word with no repeated letters?
questions.funFact: 'Uncopyrightable' uses fifteen different letters without repeating a single one.

questions.prompt: What do we call words spelled identically but with different meanings and pronunciations, like 'bow' (of a ship) and 'bow' (to bend)?
questions.funFact: Heteronyms are a special subset of homographs that are also pronounced differently.

questions.prompt: Which letter of the English alphabet is often cited as the only one that is never silent in any word?
questions.funFact: Even seemingly essential letters like 'a' or 'e' can be silent, but 'v' almost always makes a sound.

questions.prompt: What is the term for an unusually long word describing the fear of long words, often cited as ironic?
questions.funFact: The word itself is 36 letters long, poking fun at the phobia it names.

questions.prompt: What do we call a word formed by rearranging the letters of another word, such as 'listen' and 'silent'?
questions.funFact: Anagrams have been used for centuries in puzzles, pen names, and wordplay.

questions.prompt: Often cited as one of the longest words in a standard English dictionary, which 28-letter word refers to opposition to withdrawing state support for an established church?
questions.funFact: Despite its length, the word rarely comes up outside of trivia and spelling contests.

questions.prompt: Which English playwright is credited with coining or popularizing hundreds of now-common words, including 'eyeball' and 'bedroom'?
questions.funFact: Shakespeare's plays introduced or popularized an estimated 1,700+ words still used in English today.

questions.prompt: Which planet is famous for its Great Red Spot, a giant storm larger than Earth?
questions.funFact: Jupiter's Great Red Spot has been raging for at least 150 years and could fit more than one Earth inside it.

questions.prompt: Which galaxy is on a slow collision course with the Milky Way, expected to merge with it billions of years from now?
questions.funFact: The Andromeda Galaxy is the nearest large galaxy to the Milky Way and is already visible to the naked eye on a dark night.

questions.prompt: Which planet spins almost completely on its side, with an axis tilted about 98 degrees?
questions.funFact: Scientists think a massive collision billions of years ago may have knocked Uranus onto its side.

questions.prompt: What force keeps the planets in orbit around the Sun?
questions.funFact: The Sun's gravity is strong enough to hold planets in orbit even from billions of kilometers away, like Neptune.

questions.prompt: What do we call a piece of rock or metal from space that survives its fall through the atmosphere and lands on Earth's surface?
questions.funFact: Most meteorites that reach Earth's surface come from the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.

questions.prompt: Which is the smallest planet in our solar system?
questions.funFact: Mercury is just under one and a half times the diameter of Earth's Moon.

questions.prompt: What do astronauts wear to protect themselves while working outside their spacecraft?
questions.funFact: A spacesuit is essentially a personal spacecraft, providing oxygen, pressure, and temperature control.

questions.prompt: What do we call it when the Moon passes directly between the Sun and Earth, blocking the Sun's light?
questions.funFact: A total solar eclipse is only visible from a narrow path on Earth's surface at any given time.

questions.prompt: Which planet is closest in size to Earth, sometimes called Earth's sister planet?
questions.funFact: Venus is nearly the same size as Earth, but its thick atmosphere makes it the hottest planet in the solar system.

questions.prompt: What is the name for a huge cloud of gas and dust in space where new stars are often born?
questions.funFact: The Orion Nebula, visible to the naked eye, is one of the closest and brightest stellar nurseries to Earth.

questions.prompt: About how long does it take Mars to complete one orbit around the Sun compared to Earth?
questions.funFact: A Martian year lasts roughly 687 Earth days, nearly twice as long as ours.

questions.prompt: Which spacecraft became the first human-made object to travel beyond our solar system into interstellar space?
questions.funFact: Voyager 1, launched in 1977, crossed into interstellar space in 2012 and is still transmitting data today.

questions.prompt: What is the name of NASA's infrared space telescope, launched in 2021, designed to see farther into the universe than Hubble?
questions.funFact: The James Webb Space Telescope's giant mirror is made of 18 gold-coated hexagonal segments.

questions.prompt: What do astronomers call a sudden, dramatic brightening of a star caused by a thermonuclear explosion on its surface, without destroying the star?
questions.funFact: Unlike a supernova, a nova can repeat if the star keeps pulling in material from a companion star.

questions.prompt: Which spacecraft achieved the first-ever soft landing on the Moon, in 1966?
questions.funFact: The Soviet Union's Luna 9 beat the United States to a soft Moon landing by several months.

questions.prompt: What is the term for the boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing, not even light, can escape?
questions.funFact: Once anything crosses a black hole's event horizon, current physics says there is no way back out.

questions.prompt: Which planet has the longest day of any planet in the solar system, taking longer to rotate once than to orbit the Sun?
questions.funFact: A day on Venus lasts about 243 Earth days, longer than its 225-day year.

questions.prompt: In 2015, SpaceX achieved a historic milestone by landing a rocket's first stage upright after launch for the first time. What is this reusable landing technology mainly meant to reduce?
questions.funFact: Reusing rocket boosters instead of discarding them was a major step toward making spaceflight dramatically cheaper.

questions.prompt: Which planet did the Soviet Venera program successfully send probes to land on, despite its crushing atmospheric pressure and extreme heat?
questions.funFact: Venera 7 became the first spacecraft to transmit data from the surface of another planet in 1970, surviving Venus's brutal conditions only briefly.

questions.prompt: What is the name for the outer boundary of our solar system, where the Sun's solar wind is stopped by pressure from interstellar gas?
questions.funFact: Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause in 2012, officially entering interstellar space.

questions.prompt: Which Japanese spacecraft was the first in history to land on an asteroid and return a physical sample of it to Earth?
questions.funFact: Hayabusa returned tiny grains of asteroid Itokawa to Earth in 2010 after a difficult seven-year mission.

questions.prompt: What term describes the theoretical point of infinite density believed to exist at the very center of a black hole?
questions.funFact: At a singularity, the known laws of physics as we understand them break down completely.

questions.prompt: Which spacecraft became the first to fly past Pluto, sending back the first close-up images of the dwarf planet in 2015?
questions.funFact: New Horizons launched in January 2006, just months before Pluto was reclassified as a dwarf planet, and took over nine years to reach it.

questions.prompt: What observation, made by Edwin Hubble in the 1920s, showed that distant galaxies are moving away from us and provided key evidence the universe is expanding?
questions.funFact: Hubble found that the farther away a galaxy is, the faster it appears to be moving away from us.

questions.prompt: What method for detecting planets around other stars relies on measuring the slight dimming of starlight as a planet passes in front of its star?
questions.funFact: NASA's Kepler mission used the transit method to discover thousands of exoplanets.

questions.prompt: Based on its shape, what type of galaxy is the Milky Way classified as?
questions.funFact: The Milky Way has a central bar-shaped structure made of stars, with spiral arms winding outward from it.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the region just outside a black hole's event horizon where light itself can, in theory, orbit due to the immense gravity?
questions.funFact: The photon sphere lies at about 1.5 times a non-rotating black hole's Schwarzschild radius.

questions.prompt: In tennis, what is a score of zero called?
questions.funFact: The tennis term "love" for zero may come from the French word l'oeuf, meaning egg, for its round shape.

questions.prompt: How many players are on a standard indoor volleyball team on the court at one time?
questions.funFact: Each of the six volleyball players rotates positions clockwise every time their team wins back the serve.

questions.prompt: In golf, what term describes finishing a hole in one stroke under par?
questions.funFact: The term birdie comes from old American slang calling something excellent "a bird."

questions.prompt: Which sport is played by hitting a feathered shuttlecock over a net with a racket?
questions.funFact: A badminton shuttlecock can travel faster than any other object struck in a racket sport, over 400 km/h off an elite smash.

questions.prompt: In bowling, what is the term for knocking down all ten pins on three consecutive throws?
questions.funFact: The nickname "turkey" for three straight strikes dates back to early 20th-century tournaments that awarded turkeys as prizes.

questions.prompt: In which sport would a player perform a slam dunk?
questions.funFact: The slam dunk was briefly banned in U.S. college basketball from 1967 to 1976.

questions.prompt: How many holes are played in a standard round of golf?
questions.funFact: The 18-hole round became standard after the Old Course at St Andrews in Scotland settled on that number in 1764.

questions.prompt: What is the maximum number of clubs a golfer is allowed to carry in their bag during a round?
questions.funFact: The 14-club limit was introduced in the 1930s to stop wealthy players from gaining an edge by carrying dozens of clubs.

questions.prompt: Which of the four main competitive swimming strokes is generally the fastest?
questions.funFact: In competitive swimming, "freestyle" almost always means front crawl, since it's the fastest stroke a swimmer can choose.

questions.prompt: The Daytona 500 is a famous annual event in which motorsport?
questions.funFact: The Daytona 500 is considered NASCAR's most prestigious race and is often called "The Great American Race."

questions.prompt: Which Winter Olympic sport combines cross-country skiing with rifle shooting at targets?
questions.funFact: Biathlon traces its roots to Scandinavian military ski patrols who trained to travel and shoot in snowy terrain.

questions.prompt: In golf, what is the term for finishing a hole two strokes under par?
questions.funFact: Golf's under-par scoring terms follow a bird theme, with an albatross for three under par being even rarer than an eagle.

questions.prompt: In which country did the sport of table tennis originate in the late 19th century?
questions.funFact: Table tennis began as an after-dinner parlor game among Victorian-era English families using books as a net.

questions.prompt: In American football, how many points does a touchdown itself count for, before any extra point attempt?
questions.funFact: A touchdown is worth 6 points, and teams can then attempt an extra point kick or a two-point conversion.

questions.prompt: What is the name of cricket's premier international one-day tournament, contested every four years?
questions.funFact: The first Cricket World Cup was held in England in 1975, and the West Indies won the inaugural title.

questions.prompt: Which grappling-focused martial art, developed in Brazil in the 20th century, emphasizes ground fighting and submission holds?
questions.funFact: Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu grew out of techniques taught to the Gracie family by Japanese judoka in the early 1900s.

questions.prompt: In Formula 1, what do drivers earn for finishing in the top ten positions of a Grand Prix?
choices: Pole position for the next race
questions.funFact: F1's points system awards the most points to the race winner, with smaller amounts down to tenth place.

questions.prompt: What is the term used in soccer for a match that ends with both teams scoring the same number of goals?
questions.funFact: In league soccer, a draw typically earns each team one point, compared to three points for a win.

questions.prompt: Which major golf tournament, first played in 1934, is the only major always held at the same course, Augusta National?
questions.funFact: The winner of The Masters is awarded a distinctive green jacket, a tradition that began in 1949.

questions.prompt: As of the 2023 tournament, which national team has won the most Rugby World Cup titles?
questions.funFact: South Africa won its fourth Rugby World Cup title in 2023, edging past New Zealand's three championships.

questions.prompt: In cricket, what is the term for a bowler taking three wickets on three consecutive deliveries?
questions.funFact: The cricket term "hat-trick" is believed to have inspired the same term now used in hockey and soccer for three goals by one player.

questions.prompt: What is the maximum possible score in a single game of ten-pin bowling?
questions.funFact: A perfect 300 game requires bowling twelve strikes in a row across all ten frames.

questions.prompt: Which swimmer holds the record for the most Olympic gold medals ever won by an individual, with 23?
questions.funFact: Michael Phelps won 23 Olympic gold medals across four Games, from 2004 to 2016.

questions.prompt: In Olympic fencing, what are the three weapon disciplines used in competition?
questions.funFact: Each fencing weapon has different target areas and rules; sabre, for example, allows hits with the edge of the blade, not just the tip.

questions.prompt: In rugby union, how many points is a successful drop goal worth?
questions.funFact: A drop goal is scored by kicking the ball between the posts after it bounces off the ground during open play.

questions.prompt: Which classic chess opening begins with the moves 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bb5?
questions.funFact: The Ruy Lopez is named after a 16th-century Spanish priest who wrote one of the earliest detailed studies of chess strategy.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the high jump technique, where the jumper clears the bar backwards and head-first, popularized at the 1968 Olympics?
questions.funFact: Dick Fosbury's unconventional technique was considered strange at first but is now used by nearly every elite high jumper.

questions.prompt: What does the abbreviation GPS stand for?
questions.funFact: GPS relies on a network of at least 24 satellites orbiting Earth to pinpoint your location.

questions.prompt: Which company manufactures the PlayStation gaming console?
questions.funFact: The original PlayStation launched in Japan in December 1994.

questions.prompt: Bluetooth wireless technology is named after which historical figure?
questions.funFact: Bluetooth's logo combines two ancient runic letters representing the initials of Danish king Harald Bluetooth Gormsson.

questions.prompt: What does the file format abbreviation PDF stand for?
questions.funFact: PDF was created by Adobe in the early 1990s to preserve document formatting across different devices and printers.

questions.prompt: Which company developed the Android operating system used on most of the world's smartphones?
questions.funFact: Google acquired Android Inc. in 2005, about three years before the first commercial Android phone launched in 2008.

questions.prompt: What is the common term for unwanted, unsolicited bulk email messages?
questions.funFact: The term spam for junk email is widely believed to come from a Monty Python sketch about canned meat.

questions.prompt: What input device do you move across a desk to control a pointer on a computer screen?
questions.funFact: The first computer mouse, invented in the 1960s by Douglas Engelbart, had a wooden shell and only one button.

questions.prompt: Which video app known for short, vertical videos launched internationally in 2018 after merging with Musical.ly?
questions.funFact: TikTok's parent company, ByteDance, acquired the app Musical.ly in 2017 and merged it into TikTok the following year.

questions.prompt: What do we call a stored copy of your files, kept in case the originals are lost or damaged?
questions.funFact: Many IT experts recommend the "3-2-1 rule": three copies of your data, on two types of storage, with one kept offsite.

questions.prompt: What is the name for the small chip inside a mobile phone that identifies the subscriber and stores their phone number?
questions.funFact: SIM stands for Subscriber Identity Module, and the first SIM card was produced in 1991.

questions.prompt: What does HTML, the language used to structure web pages, stand for?
questions.funFact: HTML was created by Tim Berners-Lee in 1990 alongside the World Wide Web itself.

questions.prompt: Which web browser, released by Netscape in 1994, helped popularize the early World Wide Web before losing ground to Internet Explorer?
questions.funFact: Netscape Navigator's source code was later released publicly and became the foundation for the Firefox browser.

questions.prompt: What term describes a network of infected computers controlled remotely, often without their owners' knowledge, to carry out attacks?
questions.funFact: Botnets have been used to launch some of the largest denial-of-service attacks in internet history.

questions.prompt: In programming, what term describes a reusable, self-contained block of code designed to perform one specific task?
questions.funFact: Reusable functions let programmers avoid rewriting the same code every time it's needed.

questions.prompt: Which company launched the iPod, the first commercially successful digital music player of its kind, in 2001?
questions.funFact: The original iPod had a five-gigabyte hard drive and could hold about a thousand songs.

questions.prompt: In computing, what does the abbreviation IoT stand for, referring to everyday objects connected to the internet?
questions.funFact: Everyday IoT devices range from smart thermostats to internet-connected refrigerators and doorbells.

questions.prompt: What is the name of the technology that lets one physical computer run several separate operating systems or isolated environments at once?
questions.funFact: Cloud data centers rely heavily on virtualization to run many customers' workloads securely on shared hardware.

questions.prompt: Which company began in 1994 as a hand-curated directory of favorite websites, before search engines like it became algorithm-driven?
questions.funFact: Yahoo! started out literally called "Jerry and David's Guide to the World Wide Web," after its two founders.

questions.prompt: Which programming language, first released in 1991 by Guido van Rossum, is known for its readable, indentation-based syntax?
questions.funFact: Python is named after the British comedy group Monty Python, not the snake.

questions.prompt: According to computing lore, what actual insect was found stuck in a relay of the Harvard Mark II computer in 1947, contributing to the term "debugging"?
questions.funFact: Grace Hopper's team taped the moth into their logbook, noting it as the first actual case of a bug being found.

questions.prompt: Which 1971 self-replicating program, often cited as an early computer virus, spread across early ARPANET-connected systems and prompted an antivirus-like counter-program called Reaper?
questions.funFact: Creeper was more an experiment than malicious code, simply displaying a taunting message on infected terminals.

questions.prompt: Which 1960s project, funded by the U.S. Department of Defense, became the direct precursor to today's internet?
questions.funFact: ARPANET sent its first message between UCLA and Stanford in 1969.

questions.prompt: What cryptographic concept, essential to secure websites, uses a public key to encrypt data and a separate private key to decrypt it?
questions.funFact: Public-key cryptography, developed in the 1970s, underpins HTTPS connections and secure online banking today.

questions.prompt: What term describes a chip design philosophy, used in processors like ARM found in most smartphones, that relies on a small, simplified set of instructions to boost efficiency?
questions.funFact: RISC stands for Reduced Instruction Set Computer, a design approach that helps mobile chips stay power-efficient.

questions.prompt: Which early hobbyist computer, sold as a kit in 1975, is often credited with sparking the home computer revolution and inspiring Bill Gates and Paul Allen to write their first software product?
questions.funFact: Gates and Allen wrote a BASIC interpreter for the Altair 8800, which became Microsoft's very first product.

questions.prompt: Which networking protocol suite, whose name stands for Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol, forms the basic communication language of the internet?
questions.funFact: TCP/IP officially became ARPANET's standard protocol on January 1, 1983, an event engineers nicknamed "flag day."

questions.prompt: In 1997, which chess-playing computer became the first machine to beat a reigning world champion, Garry Kasparov, in a full match under standard time controls?
questions.funFact: IBM's Deep Blue beat Kasparov in a six-game rematch held in New York in May 1997.

questions.prompt: Which Dutch painter is famous for The Starry Night and for cutting off part of his own ear?
questions.funFact: Van Gogh painted The Starry Night in 1889 while staying at an asylum in southern France.

questions.prompt: Which ancient Egyptian monument, a giant limestone statue with a lion's body and a human head, sits near the pyramids of Giza?
questions.funFact: The Great Sphinx of Giza is one of the largest and oldest monumental sculptures in the world, carved around 4,500 years ago.

questions.prompt: What do we call a painting or drawing an artist makes of themselves?
questions.funFact: Rembrandt painted around 40 self-portraits in oil over his lifetime, plus dozens more as drawings and etchings.

questions.prompt: Which three colors, mixed in pairs, form the traditional artist's primary color set used to create other colors?
questions.funFact: Mixing all three traditional primary colors together in the right balance produces a muddy brown.

questions.prompt: What is a large painting made directly onto a wet plaster wall or ceiling called?
questions.funFact: Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling using the fresco technique.

questions.prompt: Which famous ceiling, painted by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1512, depicts scenes from the Book of Genesis, including The Creation of Adam?
questions.funFact: Michelangelo spent roughly four years painting the Sistine Chapel ceiling, mostly working from scaffolding just below it.

questions.prompt: Which material did ancient Greek sculptors most famously carve to create idealized statues of the human body?
questions.funFact: Many ancient Greek marble statues were originally painted in bright colors, though the paint has worn away over time.

questions.prompt: What is the name for a painting of an arrangement of objects, like fruit, flowers, or dishes, rather than people or places?
questions.funFact: Still-life painting became especially popular during the 17th-century Dutch Golden Age of art.

questions.prompt: Which Mexican painter is known for vivid self-portraits exploring identity, often featuring a unibrow and traditional Tehuana clothing?
questions.funFact: Frida Kahlo painted around 55 self-portraits over her career, once explaining she painted herself because she was so often alone.

questions.prompt: What do we call a picture made by gluing pieces of paper, fabric, or other materials onto a surface?
questions.funFact: Pablo Picasso and Georges Braque were early pioneers of collage as a fine art technique in the 1910s.

questions.prompt: In which Italian city is Michelangelo's statue of David displayed today, inside the Galleria dell'Accademia?
questions.funFact: David was moved indoors to the Accademia in 1873 to protect it from weather damage; a replica now stands at its original outdoor spot.

questions.prompt: Which art movement, emerging in early 20th-century Italy, celebrated speed, machinery, and modern technology while rejecting the art of the past?
questions.funFact: The Futurist Manifesto, published by Filippo Tommaso Marinetti in 1909, praised a racing car as more beautiful than an ancient sculpture.

questions.prompt: Which Japanese woodblock print, part of a series called Thirty-six Views of Mount Fuji, shows a giant cresting wave threatening small boats?
choices: The Dream of the Fisherman's Wife
questions.funFact: The artist Hokusai created The Great Wave around 1831, and it remains one of the most reproduced artworks in history.

questions.prompt: What term describes a sculpture carved so it projects only slightly from a flat background, rather than standing fully free?
questions.funFact: Ancient Egyptian temple walls are covered in relief carvings depicting gods and pharaohs.

questions.prompt: Which Norwegian artist painted The Scream, one of the most recognized images of existential anxiety in art history?
questions.funFact: Munch created several versions of The Scream; one was stolen from an Oslo museum in 1994 and later recovered.

questions.prompt: Which art movement, led by artists like Jackson Pollock in 1940s and 1950s New York, emphasized spontaneous, gestural brushwork on large abstract canvases?
questions.funFact: Pollock became famous for his drip paintings, made by pouring and flinging paint over canvases laid on the floor.

questions.prompt: Which pop artist and printmaker famously created silkscreen portraits of Marilyn Monroe and a series depicting Campbell's Soup Cans?
questions.funFact: Warhol's Campbell's Soup Cans series, first shown in 1962, featured 32 canvases representing each variety sold at the time.

questions.prompt: What is the term for applying paint in thick, visible strokes or layers that add physical texture to a canvas?
questions.funFact: Vincent van Gogh used heavy impasto throughout his career, giving his canvases a raised, sculptural surface.

questions.prompt: Which Baroque Italian sculptor and architect designed the sweeping colonnade in St. Peter's Square at the Vatican?
questions.funFact: Bernini's elliptical colonnade, completed in 1667, was designed to symbolically embrace visitors arriving at St. Peter's.

questions.prompt: Which term describes the soft, smoky blending of tones without harsh outlines, a technique perfected by Leonardo da Vinci?
questions.funFact: Sfumato comes from the Italian word for smoke, describing da Vinci's hazy transitions of tone visible in the Mona Lisa.

questions.prompt: Which Russian-born painter, who taught at the Bauhaus school in Germany and wrote a treatise called Point and Line to Plane, is often credited as a pioneer of abstract art?
questions.funFact: Kandinsky is often credited with painting some of the first purely abstract works in Western art, around 1910 to 1913.

questions.prompt: What painting technique, closely associated with Caravaggio, uses extreme, dramatic contrasts between light and dark to heighten emotional intensity?
questions.funFact: Caravaggio's tenebrist style influenced a generation of Baroque painters known as the Caravaggisti.

questions.prompt: Which prehistoric cave in France, discovered in 1940, contains some of the most famous Paleolithic paintings of animals like bulls and horses?
questions.funFact: The Lascaux paintings, roughly 17,000 years old, were closed to the public in 1963 to prevent damage from visitors' breath and body heat.

questions.prompt: Which movement, founded in Zurich during World War I, deliberately rejected logic and traditional aesthetics, producing nonsensical, anti-art works?
questions.funFact: Dada artist Marcel Duchamp shocked the art world by submitting a urinal, titled Fountain, as a sculpture in 1917.

questions.prompt: Which Russian painter, a founder of the Suprematist movement, created Black Square, a stark canvas featuring only a black square on a white ground?
questions.funFact: Malevich first exhibited Black Square in 1915, describing it as a new zero point for painting.

questions.prompt: Which Early Netherlandish painter created the intricate, symbol-packed triptych The Garden of Earthly Delights around 1500?
questions.funFact: Bosch's triptych is filled with fantastical creatures and has puzzled art historians for centuries.

questions.prompt: Which ancient Greek sculptor, active in the 5th century BCE, was renowned for his lost bronze statue known as the Spear-Bearer, later celebrated as an ideal of human proportion?
questions.funFact: Polykleitos wrote a treatise called the Canon, laying out mathematical ratios for the ideal depiction of the human body.

questions.prompt: What is unique about every zebra's stripe pattern?
choices: No two zebras have the exact same stripe pattern
questions.funFact: Like human fingerprints, a zebra's stripe pattern is unique to that individual animal.

questions.prompt: What is a baby kangaroo called?
questions.funFact: A newborn kangaroo, called a joey, is just a few centimeters long and crawls into its mother's pouch to keep developing.

questions.prompt: Bamboo is classified as which type of plant?
questions.funFact: Bamboo is technically a grass, and some species can grow more than 90 cm in a single day.

questions.prompt: What is the largest hot desert in the world?
questions.funFact: The Sahara covers an area roughly the size of the entire United States.

questions.prompt: What is a group of lions called?
questions.funFact: A lion pride is usually built around related females, their cubs, and a small number of resident males.

questions.prompt: Giant pandas in the wild are native to which country?
questions.funFact: Wild giant pandas live mainly in mountainous bamboo forests in central China, especially Sichuan province.

questions.prompt: Koalas are famous for sleeping how many hours a day, on average?
questions.funFact: Koalas sleep around 20 hours a day partly because their eucalyptus-leaf diet is low in energy.

questions.prompt: The baobab tree, famous for storing huge amounts of water in its trunk, is native to which continent?
questions.funFact: Baobabs are sometimes called 'upside-down trees' because their bare branches resemble roots reaching into the sky.

questions.prompt: Which is the largest species of penguin?
questions.funFact: Emperor penguins breed during the brutal Antarctic winter, with males incubating eggs on their feet for about two months.

questions.prompt: The Great Barrier Reef, the largest living structure on Earth, lies off the coast of which country?
questions.funFact: The Great Barrier Reef is so large that it is visible from space.

questions.prompt: What is the largest living species of lizard?
questions.funFact: Komodo dragons, found only on a few Indonesian islands, can grow up to 3 meters long.

questions.prompt: The platypus is unusual among mammals because it does what?
questions.funFact: The platypus is one of only a few mammals, called monotremes, that lay eggs instead of giving birth to live young.

questions.prompt: Monarch butterflies are famous for migrating thousands of miles to spend winter in which country?
questions.funFact: Monarchs that arrive in Mexico's oyamel fir forests are often several generations removed from the butterflies that started the journey north the previous spring.

questions.prompt: A narwhal's long spiral 'tusk' is actually what?
choices: A bony horn from its skull
questions.funFact: The narwhal's tusk is really an overgrown canine tooth that can grow up to 3 meters long, mostly in males.

questions.prompt: The axolotl, a salamander native to Mexico, is best known for its remarkable ability to do what?
questions.funFact: Axolotls can regrow entire limbs and even parts of their spinal cord, heart, or brain.

questions.prompt: Tardigrades, tiny eight-legged creatures, are famous for surviving which extreme environment?
choices: Only deep caves without any light
questions.funFact: In a 2007 experiment, tardigrades were exposed to the vacuum and radiation of space and survived.

questions.prompt: Flamingos get their pink color mainly from what?
choices: The mineral content of the water they live in
Pigments in the algae and shrimp they eat
questions.funFact: Flamingo chicks are actually born gray; their feathers turn pink from carotenoid pigments in their diet.

questions.prompt: The 'Great Migration' of roughly two million wildebeest takes place between the Serengeti and which other region?
questions.funFact: Each year, wildebeest and zebra travel in a huge circular route between Tanzania's Serengeti and Kenya's Maasai Mara in search of fresh grass.

questions.prompt: The towering saguaro cactus, which can live over 150 years, is native to which desert?
questions.funFact: A saguaro cactus doesn't grow its first arm until it is roughly 50 to 70 years old.

questions.prompt: Which animal is known for one of the fastest strikes of any creature relative to its size, plus extraordinarily complex eyes?
questions.funFact: A mantis shrimp's claw strike is so fast it can briefly boil the surrounding water and produce a flash of light.

questions.prompt: The star-nosed mole is notable for what remarkable ability?
choices: Seeing in complete darkness better than any mammal
Living entirely underwater its whole life
Regrowing its tail like a lizard
Identifying and eating food faster than any other mammal
questions.funFact: Using the sensitive tentacles on its nose, a star-nosed mole can identify and eat prey in a quarter of a second or less.

questions.prompt: Which shark species is the second-largest fish in the world, after the whale shark?
questions.funFact: Despite its massive size, the basking shark is a gentle filter feeder that eats plankton, not other animals.

questions.prompt: The electric eel, capable of shocks over 600 volts, is actually more closely related to which type of fish?
questions.funFact: Despite its name, the electric eel isn't a true eel at all; it belongs to the knifefish family.

questions.prompt: Roughly what share of the world's fresh water is locked up in the Antarctic ice sheet?
questions.funFact: If the entire Antarctic ice sheet melted, global sea levels would rise by tens of meters.

questions.prompt: Which animal holds the record for the longest migration of any creature on Earth?
questions.funFact: Arctic terns fly from pole to pole each year, racking up a round trip of roughly 70,000 kilometers.

questions.prompt: The numbat, a small Australian marsupial, is unusual because it feeds almost exclusively on what?
questions.funFact: Unlike most marsupials, the numbat is active during the day, when its termite prey is easiest to find.

questions.prompt: The quokka, sometimes called the 'world's happiest animal' for its smiling expression, is found mainly on which island?
questions.funFact: Quokkas on Rottnest Island, off Western Australia, have little fear of humans, which has made them popular selfie subjects.

questions.prompt: Potatoes were originally domesticated on which continent?
questions.funFact: Potatoes were first cultivated in the Andes mountains thousands of years ago before spreading worldwide.

questions.prompt: Cloves, a spice often used in mulled wine, are actually the dried what of a tropical tree?
questions.funFact: Cloves come from the unopened flower buds of a tree native to Indonesia's Maluku Islands, once called the 'Spice Islands'.

questions.prompt: Kimchi, a fermented and seasoned cabbage dish, is a national staple of which country?
questions.funFact: Traditional kimchi is fermented in earthenware jars, sometimes buried underground to keep a steady temperature.

questions.prompt: The word 'sushi' actually refers to which part of the dish, not necessarily raw fish?
questions.funFact: Because 'sushi' means vinegared rice, many types of sushi use cooked seafood or vegetables instead of raw fish.

questions.prompt: Botanically speaking, the avocado is classified as what?
questions.funFact: Avocados are technically a single-seeded berry that grows on a tree.

questions.prompt: Drinking chocolate was first developed by ancient civilizations in which region?
questions.funFact: The Aztec and Maya prepared a bitter, frothy chocolate drink from cacao long before sugar was added to it.

questions.prompt: Honey found in ancient Egyptian tombs was reportedly still edible thousands of years later because honey almost never does what?
questions.funFact: Honey's low moisture and natural acidity make it one of the only foods that essentially never spoils.

questions.prompt: Tofu, made by coagulating soy milk, was invented in which country over 2,000 years ago?
questions.funFact: Tofu is traditionally credited to ancient China, possibly during the Han dynasty.

questions.prompt: Which country produces roughly 70% of the world's maple syrup?
questions.funFact: The Canadian province of Quebec alone accounts for the large majority of global maple syrup production.

questions.prompt: Vanilla, the world's second most expensive spice after saffron, comes from a type of what plant?
questions.funFact: Vanilla pods come from a climbing orchid native to Mexico, and pollinating the flowers by hand is extremely labor-intensive.

questions.prompt: Which country is consistently the world's largest producer of coffee?
questions.funFact: Brazil has been the top coffee-producing country for more than 150 years.

questions.prompt: The Maillard reaction, responsible for the browning and flavor of seared meat and toasted bread, occurs between amino acids and what?
questions.funFact: The Maillard reaction happens when heat causes amino acids and sugars to react, creating hundreds of new flavor compounds.

questions.prompt: Injera, a spongy flatbread used to scoop up stews, is a staple of which country's cuisine?
questions.funFact: Injera is traditionally made from teff flour, a tiny gluten-free grain native to the Horn of Africa.

questions.prompt: Cardamom, sometimes called the 'queen of spices,' is native to the Western Ghats of which country?
questions.funFact: Cardamom is one of the most expensive spices by weight, prized in both sweet and savory dishes.

questions.prompt: In ceviche, raw fish is 'cooked' not with heat but through what process?
questions.funFact: The citric acid in lime or lemon juice denatures the proteins in the fish, giving it a texture similar to cooked fish.

questions.prompt: The word 'ketchup' is believed to have originated from a fermented fish or soy sauce condiment from which region?
questions.funFact: The word likely traveled from a Chinese term through Malay traders before European sailors adapted it, eventually turning it into the tomato-based sauce we know today.

questions.prompt: The pungent green paste often served as 'wasabi' outside Japan is usually made mostly from what?
questions.funFact: Real wasabi root is expensive and hard to grow, so many restaurants substitute horseradish colored with green dye.

questions.prompt: Authentic Parmigiano-Reggiano cheese can legally only be produced in specific regions of which country?
questions.funFact: Parmigiano-Reggiano has a protected designation of origin, meaning it can only be made in a handful of provinces in northern Italy.
